description: This figure presents the schematic diagrams of mechanical test specimens for Fe–28Mn–10Al–0.8C low-density steel with varying nitrogen contents (LN and HN steels). Subfigure (a) shows a cylindrical tensile specimen with a gauge diameter of Ø10 mm, gauge length of 50 mm, overall length of 114 mm, grip section diameter of Ø16 mm, grip length of 20 mm, and a fillet radius of R10 mm connecting the grip and gauge sections. Subfigure (b) depicts a Charpy V-notch impact specimen with dimensions of 50 mm × 10 mm × 10 mm and a 45° V-notch, leaving a remaining height of 8 mm below the notch. Caption states these specimens were used for tensile and impact tests. According to the citation context, tensile tests were performed at room temperature on a WDW-200 universal testing machine at a strain rate of 1×10⁻³ s⁻¹ and a crosshead speed of 3 mm min⁻¹, yielding stress–strain curves corrected for elastic deformation, while impact toughness was measured using a JB-300B impact tester to obtain Charpy V-notch (CVN) energy. These standardized specimen geometries enable consistent evaluation of how nitrogen addition influences yield strength, ultimate tensile strength, elongation, reduction of area, and impact toughness in the studied low-density steels.

This figure presents room-temperature engineering stress–strain curves for low-nitrogen (LN) and high-nitrogen (HN) variants of Fe–28Mn–10Al–0.8C low-density steel. Six replicate curves are shown: LN1 (green dashed), LN2 (grey dashed), and LN3 (yellow dotted) for the LN steel, and HN1 (black solid), HN2 (red solid), and HN3 (blue solid) for the HN steel. All specimens exhibit continuous yielding followed by pronounced strain hardening and necking prior to fracture. Visually, the HN steel curves extend to markedly higher strains before fracture, whereas the LN curves terminate at lower elongations, indicating earlier failure during local necking. The accompanying caption and text report specific mechanical values: the HN steel achieves an ultimate tensile strength of 841.5 MPa and an elongation of 74.5%, compared with 832.5 MPa and 62.5% for the LN steel, while the yield strength decreases from 685 MPa to 647 MPa due to reduced ferrite content with nitrogen addition. The consistently larger area under the HN curves indicates greater energy absorption during fracture. These tensile results support the core conclusion that nitrogen addition refines austenite grains and simultaneously enhances ultimate strength, plasticity, and fracture energy while lowering yield strength in the studied low-density steel system.

Figure 3 presents optical micrographs comparing the microstructures of LN (low-nitrogen) and HN (high-nitrogen) Fe–28Mn–10Al–0.8C low-density steels after vacuum induction melting, homogenization at 1423 K for 2 h, multidirectional hot forging between 1123 and 1323 K, and air cooling. Subfigure (a) shows the LN steel comprising an austenite matrix with coarse, long parallel stripe-like ferrite located at austenite boundaries; the caption states a mean austenite grain size of 20.5 μm and a ferrite area percentage of 5.08%. Subfigure (b) shows the HN steel with a refined austenite matrix and markedly less ferrite; the caption reports a mean grain size of 12.1 μm and a ferrite area percentage of 1.84%. Both micrographs reveal annealing twins intersecting within austenite grains. The comparison demonstrates that nitrogen addition significantly refines austenite grains and reduces ferrite content, consistent with the reported increase in fine deformation twins in HN steel. These microstructural changes support the conclusion that nitrogen stabilizes austenite, suppresses ferrite formation, and thereby enhances ultimate tensile strength, plasticity, and toughness, while the reduced ferrite fraction contributes to the observed slight decrease in yield strength.

The figure presents two scanning electron microscopy (SEM) micrographs showing typical morphologies of aluminum nitride (AlN) inclusions in high-nitrogen (HN) Fe–28Mn–10Al–0.8C low-density steel. Panel (a) displays an intact, polygonal AlN inclusion with straight edges and sharp angles, exhibiting a characteristic hexagonal morphology; the scale bar indicates 2 μm. Panel (b) shows a fractured or broken AlN inclusion, which caption and citation report resulted from the multidirectional hot forging process (1123–1323 K). The image demonstrates that while fine AlN inclusions (84% smaller than 2 μm) act as heterogeneous nucleation sites for austenite grain refinement, aggregated or fractured AlN inclusions can serve as crack initiation sites during deformation. This supports the core finding that nitrogen addition refines austenite grains and enhances mechanical properties, yet forged AlN inclusions constitute critical fracture origins in the HN steel.

Figure 4 presents scanning electron microscopy (SEM) micrographs showing the typical morphologies of non-metallic inclusions in low-nitrogen (LN) Fe–28Mn–10Al–0.8C low-density steel. Panel (a) shows an elliptical Mn(Mg)S sulfide inclusion with a smooth edge, captured with a 2 μm scale bar. Panel (b) displays a polyhedral AlN inclusion exhibiting a faceted structure, with a 1 μm scale bar. Panel (c) reveals a composite AlN–Mn(Mg)S inclusion in which the sulfide precipitated on the surface of an AlN core, indicated by arrows and shown with a 2 μm scale bar. These images collectively demonstrate that the dominant inclusions in the LN steel consist of individual AlN, individual Mn(Mg)S, and their composite aggregates. The distinct morphological contrast between the elliptical sulfide and the polyhedral nitride is clearly resolved. According to the caption and related citation context, these inclusions were generated during the solidification and smelting processes, and their characteristics provide essential evidence for understanding microstructural evolution and mechanical behavior, particularly the propensity of AlN inclusions to become crack origins after forging.

This stacked bar chart presents the statistical number percentage (NP) distributions of non-metallic inclusions across different size intervals for the low-nitrogen (LN) and high-nitrogen (HN) variants of Fe–28Mn–10Al–0.8C low-density steel. The two vertical bars correspond to the LN and HN samples, segmented by inclusion diameter ranges: <2 μm, 2–4 μm, 4–6 μm, and >6 μm. For the LN steel, the respective number percentages read directly from the chart are 39 %, 39 %, 12 %, and 10 %. By contrast, nitrogen addition in the HN steel dramatically refines the inclusion population, yielding 84 % <2 μm, 11 % 2–4 μm, 4 % 4–6 μm, and only a negligible fraction above 6 μm. The accompanying caption and text report that the dominant inclusions in the HN steel are fine AlN particles, which act as heterogeneous nucleation sites that refine austenite grains during solidification. Nevertheless, the text also states that aggregated AlN inclusions generated during smelting can serve as crack initiation sites, potentially compromising fracture toughness despite the overall grain-refining benefit.

This bar chart compares the size distribution and number density of non-metallic inclusions in low-nitrogen (LN) and high-nitrogen (HN) Fe–28Mn–10Al–0.8C low-density steels. The y-axis reports inclusion number density (# mm⁻²) across size classes from <1 μm to 7–8 μm. The HN steel exhibits dramatically higher inclusion densities in the sub-micrometer and 1–2 μm ranges, peaking near 72 # mm⁻² at 1–2 μm, compared to LN steel which shows a broader distribution with lower peak values around 20 # mm⁻² in the 1–2 and 2–3 μm ranges. Conversely, LN steel contains relatively more inclusions in the 2–4 μm and 7–8 μm categories. These statistical results indicate that nitrogen addition substantially increases the total number density of inclusions while shifting the size distribution toward finer particles. As reported in the associated text, the dominant inclusion in HN steel is AlN, 84% of which is smaller than 2 μm, and these fine inclusions promote austenite grain refinement by acting as heterogeneous nucleation sites.

Figure 8 presents the AlN stability diagram for Fe–28Mn–10Al–0.8C steels calculated at three temperatures, with [Al] (wt%) on the horizontal axis and [N] (wt%) on the vertical axis. The solid, dotted, and dash-dotted curves represent the equilibrium solubility limits at the smelting temperature (1873 K), liquidus (1587 K), and solidus (1493 K), respectively, dividing the diagram into "AlN saturation" and "No AlN" regions. Two alloy compositions are marked: LN steel at (9.90, 0.0025) lies well below the 1873 K line in the No AlN zone, indicating AlN is thermodynamically unstable during smelting; HN steel at (9.95, 0.1326) falls far above the saturation line, meaning AlN inclusions readily form and remain stable during solidification. This thermodynamic analysis explains the experimental observation that AlN becomes the dominant inclusion type only in the high-nitrogen variant.

This figure presents the calculated pseudobinary phase diagram of the Fe–28Mn–10Al–0.8C–N system, computed using Thermo-Calc software with the TCFE8 database. The vertical axis shows temperature ranging from approximately 700 K to 1800 K, while the horizontal axis represents nitrogen mass fraction from 0.0 to 2.0 wt%. The diagram delineates multiple phase fields including liquid (L), ferrite (delta), austenite (gamma), alpha-phase, and various precipitates such as AlN, M5C2, and M23C6. Key phase regions identified include L+AlN, L+delta+AlN, delta+gamma+AlN, gamma+AlN, gamma+AlN+M5C2, and delta+alpha+AlN+M23C6, among others. As nitrogen content increases, the austenite phase field significantly expands toward higher temperatures and broader composition ranges, while ferrite gradually diminishes and eventually disappears. AlN remains stable across a wide temperature and composition range. The diagram supports the experimental observation that nitrogen acts as a strong austenite stabilizer, promotes austenite nucleation, and suppresses ferrite growth, thereby explaining the reduced ferrite content and refined grain structure observed in high-nitrogen low-density steel.

Figure 10 presents the tensile fracture morphologies of Fe–28Mn–10Al–0.8C low-density steels with different nitrogen contents: (a) LN steel (0.0025 wt% N) and (b) HN steel (0.1326 wt% N). Each panel includes a three-dimensional surface-height map (left, with height scales up to 12.19 mm for LN and 8.09 mm), a low-magnification SEM image of the whole fracture surface (middle, 1 mm scale bar), and a high-magnification SEM image of the dimpled region (right, 5 μm scale bar). For the LN steel, the macroscopic fracture surface is oriented at approximately 45° to the loading direction, indicating shear-stress-dominated failure, and the high-magnification SEM reveals smaller, more numerous dimples signifying severe local stress concentration. In contrast, the HN steel exhibits a crater-shaped fracture with considerably greater macroscopic necking; its high-magnification SEM shows larger, deeper, and more uniformly distributed dimples surrounded by fine dimples, characteristic of superior plasticity and fracture toughness. These observations support the conclusion that nitrogen addition refines austenite grains and promotes deformation twins, thereby homogenizing deformation, relieving stress concentration, and hindering crack propagation.

Figure 11 compares impact fracture morphologies of Fe–28Mn–10Al–0.8C low-density steels: low-nitrogen (LN, 0.0025 wt% N) in (a) and high-nitrogen (HN, 0.1326 wt% N) in (b), both processed by vacuum induction melting and multidirectional hot forging. Each row comprises a 3D fracture surface height map (color scale in mm), a low-magnification SEM overview (scale bar 1 mm), and a high-magnification SEM micrograph of the rupture surface (scale bar 10 μm). The LN steel profile peaks at 7.53 mm; its low-magnification SEM shows a standard ductile impact fracture with fibrous, radial, and shear-lip zones, while its high-magnification SEM reveals elongated parabolic dimples aligned along the crack propagation direction, indicating shear-assisted fast crack growth. The HN steel profile peaks at 9.95 mm; its low-magnification SEM exhibits a more pronounced shear-lip zone, and its high-magnification SEM displays larger, deeper, and more equiaxed dimples, signifying greater plastic deformation and higher energy absorption during impact. These observations support that nitrogen addition refines austenite grains, reduces grain-boundary ferrite, and markedly improves impact toughness by hindering crack propagation.

Figure 12 presents scanning electron microscopy (SEM) micrographs of typical non-metallic inclusions observed within dimples on fracture surfaces of Fe–28Mn–10Al–0.8C low-density steel. Subfigure (a), at 10 μm scale, shows Mn(Mg)S sulfide inclusions indicated by arrows; caption states these sulfides deform readily but possess weak interfacial bonding with the matrix, promoting crack initiation at the inclusion–matrix interface. Subfigure (b), at 2 μm scale, reveals aggregated AlN inclusions marked by arrows inside a dimple, which caption reports as brittle, high-melting-point phases generated during smelting. The figure underpins the key conclusion that inclusions serve as fracture origins: sulfides detach easily due to poor interfacial strength, whereas aggregated AlN clusters in high-nitrogen steel create stress concentrations. This contrast highlights how inclusion type and morphology govern crack nucleation and the resulting fracture micromechanisms in low-density steels with differing nitrogen contents.
